params.description: Three bags appear on screen, each containing 4 items.

beats.text: In our Warmup, you saw that graph scales are equal groups. But equal groups show up everywhere, not just in graphs. Today you're going to learn the name for these groups, see them in all kinds of pictures, and discover a new math symbol that makes them easy to write down. Check this out.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, each containing 4 items.

beats.text: Let me show you how I figure out the groups here. First, I count the bags.

params.description: Bags highlight sequentially as they are counted: 1, 2, 3.

beats.text: First, count the groups. How many bags? 1, 2, 3. Three bags. These are my groups.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, all highlighted. Each contains 4 items.

params.description: First bag zooms in or highlights. Items inside are counted: 1, 2, 3, 4.

beats.text: Next, look inside one bag. How many? 1, 2, 3, 4. Four items in each bag. That's how many are in each group.

elements.description: Three bags on screen. First bag highlighted, showing 4 items counted inside.

beats.text: So I have three groups of four. Say it with me: three groups of four.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, each containing 4 items.

params.description: Equation 3 × 4 appears below the bags.

beats.text: We can write three groups of four like this: 3 × 4.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially (1, 2), then one box zooms and items inside count (1, 2, 3, 4, 5).

beats.text: Let me show you the whole process. First, I count the boxes: 1, 2. Two groups. Then I look inside one box and count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five in each. So I have 2 groups of 5. That's how we name equal groups: groups first, then how many in each. The answer is 2 groups of 5.

elements.description: Equal groups visualization. 2 boxes, 5 items in each box. Reading mode.

beats.text: Here's how it works. 3 in each means every bag gets 3 items. I set the number to 3, and now all 4 bags have 3 items each. That's 4 groups of 3, which we write as 4 × 3.

beats.text: Look. Here's how we write it: 4 × 3. The 4 is the groups. The 3 is how many in each. When we multiply, we're counting equal groups.
